title: CompTIA A+ V15 Applied IT Support and Troubleshooting
description: Beginning IT support learners who need a complete, safe, evidence-driven path from first support interaction through current CompTIA A+ V15 Core 1 and Core 2 coverage and independently defended troubleshooting practice. This original, research-backed path uses repeated retrieval, cumulative artifacts, changed-case labs, stakeholder projects, and a performance exam.
outcomes: Demonstrate every unique objective in current comptia a+ v15 core 1 220-1201 and core 2 220-1202, plus the official troubleshooting methodology competency standard taught before technical changes through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate cumulative support practice across hardware, mobile, printers, networking, virtualization, cloud, windows, macos, linux, chromeos, android, applications, security, incident response, operations, communication, accessibility, privacy, sustainability, and performance-task defense through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate two hundred observable competencies, six materially different stakeholder projects, misconception-targeted theory, guided workshops, faded debugging, changed-device labs, delayed retrieval, scenario quizzes, and one unfamiliar cumulative examination through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.

Demonstrate original deterministic support-evidence manifests that make authorization, safety, baseline, hypothesis, test, observation, rollback, verification, prevention, escalation, documentation, and transfer limits mechanically reviewable through an independently verified stakeholder artifact.
sharedRequirements: Every activity retrieves user impact, asset and environment identity, authorization, safety, baseline, recent change, privacy, accessibility, least-destructive testing, evidence provenance, rollback, verification, documentation, escalation, and transfer boundaries before adding complexity.

Fictional browser cases never execute commands, inspect the host, connect to devices or networks, access accounts, change configuration, handle malware, sanitize media, open equipment, contact people, or imply authorization for real work.

Passing work requires a prediction, bounded discriminating test, observed result, changed case, rejected plausible failure, causal repair, side-effect check, prevention or recurrence plan, accessible user communication, and explicit remaining uncertainty.

Windows, Apple, Android, Linux distribution, firmware, wireless, USB, cloud, security, recycling, licensing, privacy, incident, and exam details can change; learners must name the version, date, jurisdiction, owner, and authoritative source for consequential transfer decisions.
Exam objective coverage sets minimum breadth, not maximum competence. Learners must connect each fact to a safe support decision and observable mastery evidence rather than memorize labels alone.
